  1697ŌåÆ
  1698ŌåÆWorld "MeetFin"
  1699ŌåÆLevel 23
  1700ŌåÆ
  1701ŌåÆTitle "Boss: Fin Fundamentals"
  1702ŌåÆ
  1703ŌåÆIntroduction "
  1704ŌåÆ# Boss: Putting It All Together
  1705ŌåÆ
  1706ŌåÆTime to combine everything you've learned in a two-part challenge.
  1707ŌåÆ
  1708ŌåÆ**Part 1** ŌĆö *Transitivity through extensionality*: If `x` and `y`
  1709ŌåÆhave the same value, and `y = z` as Fin elements, then `x = z`. This
  1710ŌåÆcombines `ext` on the goal (Level 15) with `rw [Fin.ext_iff] at h`
  1711ŌåÆon hypotheses (Level 7).
  1712ŌåÆ
  1713ŌåÆ**Part 2** ŌĆö *Non-triviality*: Fin 3 has more than one element.
  1714ŌåÆThis requires exhaustive case analysis (Levels 16ŌĆō18), existential
  1715ŌåÆwitnesses (Level 22), inequality proving (Level 21), and the Fin
  1716ŌåÆcontradiction pattern (Level 13) for the impossible branch.
  1717ŌåÆ
  1718ŌåÆThe boss tests whether you can assemble these moves into a
  1719ŌåÆcoherent proof. No single level taught all of this at once.
  1720ŌåÆ"
  1721ŌåÆ
  1722ŌåÆ/-- Fin equality is transitive through values, and Fin 3 is non-trivial. -/
  1723ŌåÆStatement : (ŌłĆ x y z : Fin 3, x.val = y.val ŌåÆ y = z ŌåÆ x = z) Ōł¦
  1724ŌåÆ            (ŌłĆ x : Fin 3, Ōłā y : Fin 3, x ŌēĀ y) := by
  1725ŌåÆ  Hint "The goal is a conjunction. Use `constructor` to split it
  1726ŌåÆ  into two sub-goals."
  1727ŌåÆ  constructor
  1728ŌåÆ  -- Part 1: transitivity through extensionality
  1729ŌåÆ  ┬Ę Hint "Start with `intro x y z h1 h2`."
  1730ŌåÆ    intro x y z h1 h2
  1731ŌåÆ    Hint "You need to show `x = z`. Use `ext` to convert this to
  1732ŌåÆ    a value equality `x.val = z.val`."
  1733ŌåÆ    ext
  1734ŌåÆ    Hint "Now you need `x.val = z.val`. Use `rw [Fin.ext_iff] at h2`
  1735ŌåÆ    to extract `y.val = z.val` from `{h2}`."
  1736ŌåÆ    rw [Fin.ext_iff] at h2
  1737ŌåÆ    Hint "Now `{h1}` says `x.val = y.val` and `{h2}` says
  1738ŌåÆ    `y.val = z.val`. `omega` can chain these."
  1739ŌåÆ    omega
  1740ŌåÆ  -- Part 2: non-triviality via exhaustive case analysis
  1741ŌåÆ  ┬Ę Hint "Perform exhaustive case analysis: destructure `x` with
  1742ŌåÆ    `cases`, then split on the value. For each valid case (0, 1, 2),
  1743ŌåÆ    choose a different `Fin 3` element as witness and prove inequality
  1744ŌåÆ    with `intro h; cases h`. The impossible case (value >= 3) closes
  1745ŌåÆ    with `absurd`."
  1746ŌåÆ    intro x
  1747ŌåÆ    Hint (hidden := true) "Destructure `x`: `cases x with | mk v hv =>`"
  1748ŌåÆ    cases x with
  1749ŌåÆ    | mk v hv =>
  1750ŌåÆ      Hint (hidden := true) "Case-split on `v`:
  1751ŌåÆ      `cases v with | zero => ... | succ n => ...`"
  1752ŌåÆ      cases v with
  1753ŌåÆ      | zero =>
  1754ŌåÆ        Hint (hidden := true) "The element is `0`. Provide `1` as witness:
  1755ŌåÆ        `use Ō¤©1, by omegaŌ¤®`, then prove `0 ŌēĀ 1` with `intro h; cases h`."
  1756ŌåÆ        use Ō¤©1, by omegaŌ¤®
  1757ŌåÆ        intro h
  1758ŌåÆ        cases h
  1759ŌåÆ      | succ n =>
  1760ŌåÆ        Hint (hidden := true) "The element has value `n + 1`. Split on `n`."
  1761ŌåÆ        cases n with
  1762ŌåÆ        | zero =>
  1763ŌåÆ          Hint (hidden := true) "The element is `1`. Use `Ō¤©0, by omegaŌ¤®`
  1764ŌåÆ          as witness, then `intro h; cases h`."
  1765ŌåÆ          use Ō¤©0, by omegaŌ¤®
  1766ŌåÆ          intro h
  1767ŌåÆ          cases h
  1768ŌåÆ        | succ m =>
  1769ŌåÆ          Hint (hidden := true) "The element has value `m + 2`. Split on `m`."
  1770ŌåÆ          cases m with
  1771ŌåÆ          | zero =>
  1772ŌåÆ            Hint (hidden := true) "The element is `2`. Use `Ō¤©0, by omegaŌ¤®`
  1773ŌåÆ            as witness, then `intro h; cases h`."
  1774ŌåÆ            use Ō¤©0, by omegaŌ¤®
  1775ŌåÆ            intro h
  1776ŌåÆ            cases h
  1777ŌåÆ          | succ k =>
  1778ŌåÆ            Hint (hidden := true) "Here `v = k + 3` but `hv` says `v < 3`.
  1779ŌåÆ            This is impossible: `exact absurd hv (by omega)`."
  1780ŌåÆ            exact absurd hv (by omega)
  1781ŌåÆ
  1782ŌåÆConclusion "
  1783ŌåÆCongratulations ŌĆö you've completed **Meet Fin**!
  1784ŌåÆ
  1785ŌåÆYou now have these core moves for working with `Fin n`:
  1786ŌåÆ
  1787ŌåÆ| Move | Tool | Learned in |
  1788ŌåÆ|---|---|---|
  1789ŌåÆ| Construct a `Fin n` element | `Ō¤©k, by omegaŌ¤®` | Level 1 |
  1790ŌåÆ| Extract the number / coercion | `.val`, `.isLt`, `Ōåæi` | Level 2 |
  1791ŌåÆ| Upper bound proof | `.isLt` + `omega` | Level 3 |
  1792ŌåÆ| Combine bounds from multiple elements | multiple `.isLt` + `omega` | Level 4 |
  1793ŌåÆ| Prove `Fin` elements equal | `rw [Fin.ext_iff]` or `ext` | Levels 5, 15 |
  1794ŌåÆ| Rewrite hypotheses | `rw [...] at h` | Levels 7ŌĆō8 |
  1795ŌåÆ| Derive Fin.eta | `rw [Fin.ext_iff]; rfl` | Level 10 |
  1796ŌåÆ| Proof irrelevance | `Fin.ext_iff` + `omega` | Level 11 |
  1797ŌåÆ| General `Fin (n+1)` reasoning | `.isLt` + `omega` | Level 12 |
  1798ŌåÆ| Fin contradiction pattern | `.isLt` + `omega` on empty/impossible | Level 13 |
  1799ŌåÆ| Handle `Fin 1` (singleton) | `ext` + `omega` | Level 15 |
  1800ŌåÆ| Destructure and case-split | `cases` (nested) + `absurd` | Levels 16ŌĆō18 |
  1801ŌåÆ| Modular arithmetic | construction + `rfl` | Level 19 |
  1802ŌåÆ| Use Fin as a concrete index | case analysis on indexed data | Level 20 |
  1803ŌåÆ| Prove `Fin` inequality | `intro h; cases h` | Level 21 |
  1804ŌåÆ| Existential witness | `use Ō¤©k, by omegaŌ¤®` | Level 22 |
  1805ŌåÆ
  1806ŌåÆ**Where this is going**: `Fin n` is the *canonical* finite type in
  1807ŌåÆLean ŌĆö the standard model that everything connects back to. Every
  1808ŌåÆfinite type with `n` elements is equivalent to `Fin n`, making it
  1809ŌåÆthe universal representative. A function `Fin n ŌåÆ ╬▒` is essentially
  1810ŌåÆan `n`-tuple: an ordered list of `n` elements from `╬▒`. You'll
  1811ŌåÆexplore this idea in the FinTuples world.
  1812ŌåÆ
  1813ŌåÆBeyond tuples, you'll meet `Finset` (unordered finite collections),
  1814ŌåÆ`Fintype` (types with finitely many elements), and big operators
  1815ŌåÆlike `Ōłæ` and `ŌłÅ` over finite index sets. All of these are built on
  1816ŌåÆthe foundation you've just learned.
  1817ŌåÆ
  1818ŌåÆ(As you saw in Level 19, `Fin n` also carries modular arithmetic ŌĆö
  1819ŌåÆaddition and multiplication wrap around mod `n`, making `Fin n` a
  1820ŌåÆmodel of `Ōäż/nŌäż`. This course focuses on `Fin` as a finite index
  1821ŌåÆtype, not as a number system.)
  1822ŌåÆ
  1823ŌåÆIn the next world, you'll learn to **navigate** within `Fin n` ŌĆö
  1824ŌåÆmoving forward with `Fin.succ`, embedding with `Fin.castSucc`, and
  1825ŌåÆreaching the boundary with `Fin.last`.
  1826ŌåÆ"
  1827ŌåÆ

file.content:
a value of type `╬▒` to each index `0, 1, ..., n-1`. You'll meet tuples
formally in the FinTuples world, but let's preview the idea.

**Key fact**: A function on `Fin 3` is completely determined by its
three values ŌĆö `f 0`, `f 1`, and `f 2`. If two functions agree on
these three inputs, they agree everywhere. This is because `Fin 3`
has exactly three elements (as you proved in Level 18).

**Your task**: Prove that two functions `f g : Fin 3 ŌåÆ ŌäĢ` are
equal on all inputs if they agree at positions 0, 1, and 2.

This requires case analysis: for each possible input `i : Fin 3`,
determine which of the three hypotheses closes the goal.
"

/-- A function on Fin 3 is determined by its three values. -/
Statement (f g : Fin 3 ŌåÆ ŌäĢ) (h0 : f 0 = g 0) (h1 : f 1 = g 1)
    (h2 : f 2 = g 2) : ŌłĆ i : Fin 3, f i = g i := by
  Hint "Start by introducing `i`, then destructure it:
  `intro i; cases i with | mk v hlt =>`"
  intro i
  Hint (hidden := true) "`cases i with | mk v hlt =>`"
  cases i with
  | mk v hlt =>
    Hint "Case-split on `v` to determine which position `i` is at.
    For each valid case (0, 1, 2), one of the three hypotheses
    closes the goal."
    cases v with
    | zero =>
      Hint "Here `i` is at position 0 (value `v = 0`).
      One of the three hypotheses `h0`, `h1`, `h2` matches.
      Use `exact h0`."
      exact h0
    | succ n =>
      Hint (hidden := true) "Case-split on `n`."
      cases n with
      | zero =>
        Hint (hidden := true) "Here `i` is at position 1. `exact h1` closes the goal."
        exact h1
      | succ m =>
        Hint (hidden := true) "Case-split on `m`."
        cases m with
        | zero =>
          Hint (hidden := true) "Here `i` is at position 2. `exact h2` closes the goal."
          exact h2
        | succ k =>
          Hint (hidden := true) "Impossible case: `exact absurd hlt (by omega)`."
          exact absurd hlt (by omega)

Conclusion "
This is the fundamental property of `Fin n` as an index type: since
`Fin n` has exactly `n` elements, any predicate on `Fin n` can be
verified by checking each element. A function `Fin n ŌåÆ ╬▒` is
determined by `n` values, and two such functions agree if and only
if they agree on all `n` inputs.

This is how `Fin n` connects to the concept of an **n-tuple**: the
function `f : Fin 3 ŌåÆ ŌäĢ` is just the triple `(f 0, f 1, f 2)`. Two
triples are equal when they agree position by position. You'll
formalize this connection in the FinTuples world.

In the next level, you'll learn to prove that two `Fin` elements
are *not* equal ŌĆö the inequality pattern.
"

import Game.Metadata

World "MeetFin"
Level 21

Title "Proving Inequality"

Introduction "
# Proving Two Fin Elements Are Not Equal

In Level 17, you used `absurd` when a bound was violated ŌĆö the value
was too large but the bound said it must be small. Here the situation
is different: the *constructors themselves* don't match (like `0 = 1`),
and a simpler pattern handles this case.

How do you prove `a ŌēĀ b` for `Fin` elements? In Lean, `a ŌēĀ b` is
defined as `a = b ŌåÆ False` ŌĆö a function that takes an equality proof
and derives a contradiction.

The strategy is:
1. `intro h` ŌĆö assume `a = b` (giving `h : a = b`)
2. `cases h` ŌĆö Lean checks if the constructors match; if the underlying
   values differ, it closes the goal automatically

When `h : Ō¤©a, _Ō¤® = Ō¤©b, _Ō¤®` and `a ŌēĀ b` as natural numbers, `cases h`
performs *injection* ŌĆö it sees the values inside the constructors don't
match and derives a contradiction. This is the same idea as `Fin.ext_iff`
in reverse: if the values differ, the elements can't be equal.

**Your task**: Prove that `0` and `1` are different elements of `Fin 3`.
"

/-- `0` and `1` are distinct elements of `Fin 3`. -/
Statement : (0 : Fin 3) ŌēĀ 1 := by
  Hint "Remember, `ŌēĀ` means `= ŌåÆ False`. Start with `intro h`
  to assume they are equal."
  intro h
  Hint "Now `{h}` says these `Fin` elements are equal. But their
  underlying values are `0` and `1` ŌĆö different natural numbers.
  `cases h` performs injection and sees the contradiction."
  Hint (hidden := true) "Try `cases h`."
  cases h

Conclusion "
The pattern for proving `a ŌēĀ b` on `Fin` elements is:
```
intro h
cases h
```

This is called **constructor discrimination**: when `cases` examines
an equality `h : Ō¤©a, _Ō¤® = Ō¤©b, _Ō¤®`, it performs *injection* (extracts
that `a = b`) and then checks whether this is consistent. When `a`
and `b` are distinct constructors (like `Nat.zero` and `Nat.succ _`),
the equality is impossible, and the goal closes automatically. This
pattern works for any type with distinct constructors ŌĆö `Nat`, `Bool`,
`Fin`, etc. ŌĆö not just `Fin`.

You could also prove this using `Fin.ext_iff`: `intro h; rw [Fin.ext_iff] at h; omega`.
This reduces the `Fin` equality to `0 = 1` at the `ŌäĢ` level, then
`omega` sees the contradiction. Both approaches work ŌĆö here's when
to choose each:

- **`intro h; cases h`** ŌĆö use when both sides are **concrete literals**
  (like `0` and `1`). The constructor discrimination is automatic.
- **`intro h; rw [Fin.ext_iff] at h; omega`** ŌĆö use when either side
  involves a **variable** or **arithmetic expression** (like `i` or
  `i + 1`). The `cases` approach won't fire on variable expressions,
  but reducing to values and using `omega` always works.

This is the discrimination step you'll need in the boss.

**Looking ahead**: The fact that Lean can *computationally* determine
whether two `Fin` elements are equal is called **decidable equality**.
The `intro h; cases h` pattern works because Lean's kernel can evaluate
whether the underlying constructors match. This property ŌĆö `DecidableEq
(Fin n)` ŌĆö is what will make `Finset (Fin n)` possible in Phase 2: a
finite set needs to check for duplicates, and decidable equality is what
makes that check computable.
"

World "MeetFin"
Level 22

Title "Existential Witnesses"

Introduction "
# Providing a Fin Element as a Witness

In Level 1, you constructed a `Fin` element with `exact Ō¤©k, by omegaŌ¤®`.
But what if the goal asks you to prove that *some* element exists
with a property? Then you need `use` to provide the witness.

When the goal is `Ōłā x : Fin n, P x`, the tactic `use Ō¤©k, by omegaŌ¤®`
provides a specific `Fin n` element as the witness, then asks you
to prove `P` holds for that element.

This combines two skills you already know:
- `use` from the NNG4 prerequisites (providing existential witnesses)
- `Ō¤©k, by omegaŌ¤®` from Level 1 (constructing `Fin` elements)

**Your task**: Find a `Fin 5` element whose value is `3`.
"

/-- There exists an element of `Fin 5` with value `3`. -/
Statement : Ōłā x : Fin 5, x.val = 3 := by
  Hint "Provide the witness with `use Ō¤©3, by omegaŌ¤®`. This constructs
  the element `3 : Fin 5` and provides it as the existential witness.
  The remaining goal `3 = 3` closes automatically."
  Hint (hidden := true) "`use Ō¤©3, by omegaŌ¤®` provides the witness and
  Lean closes `3 = 3` by reflexivity."
  use Ō¤©3, by omegaŌ¤®

Conclusion "
The pattern `use Ō¤©k, by omegaŌ¤®` provides a `Fin` element as an
existential witness. This is simply the combination of two moves:

| Move | From |
|---|---|
| `use expr` ŌĆö provide a witness | NNG4 prerequisites |
| `Ō¤©k, by omegaŌ¤®` ŌĆö construct a Fin element | Level 1 |

You'll use this combination in the boss.
"

import Game.Metadata

World "MeetFin"
Level 23

Title "Boss: Fin Fundamentals"

Introduction "
# Boss: Putting It All Together

Time to combine everything you've learned in a two-part challenge.

**Part 1** ŌĆö *Transitivity through extensionality*: If `x` and `y`
have the same value, and `y = z` as Fin elements, then `x = z`. This
combines `ext` on the goal (Level 15) with `rw [Fin.ext_iff] at h`
on hypotheses (Level 7).

**Part 2** ŌĆö *Non-triviality*: Fin 3 has more than one element.
This requires exhaustive case analysis (Levels 16ŌĆō18), existential
witnesses (Level 22), inequality proving (Level 21), and the Fin
contradiction pattern (Level 13) for the impossible branch.

The boss tests whether you can assemble these moves into a
coherent proof. No single level taught all of this at once.
"

/-- Fin equality is transitive through values, and Fin 3 is non-trivial. -/
Statement : (ŌłĆ x y z : Fin 3, x.val = y.val ŌåÆ y = z ŌåÆ x = z) Ōł¦
            (ŌłĆ x : Fin 3, Ōłā y : Fin 3, x ŌēĀ y) := by
  Hint "The goal is a conjunction. Use `constructor` to split it
  into two sub-goals."
  constructor
  -- Part 1: transitivity through extensionality
  ┬Ę Hint "Start with `intro x y z h1 h2`."
    intro x y z h1 h2
    Hint "You need to show `x = z`. Use `ext` to convert this to
    a value equality `x.val = z.val`."
    ext
    Hint "Now you need `x.val = z.val`. Use `rw [Fin.ext_iff] at h2`
    to extract `y.val = z.val` from `{h2}`."
    rw [Fin.ext_iff] at h2
    Hint "Now `{h1}` says `x.val = y.val` and `{h2}` says
    `y.val = z.val`. `omega` can chain these."
    omega
  -- Part 2: non-triviality via exhaustive case analysis
  ┬Ę Hint "Perform exhaustive case analysis: destructure `x` with
    `cases`, then split on the value. For each valid case (0, 1, 2),
    choose a different `Fin 3` element as witness and prove inequality
    with `intro h; cases h`. The impossible case (value >= 3) closes
    with `absurd`."
    intro x
    Hint (hidden := true) "Destructure `x`: `cases x with | mk v hv =>`"
    cases x with
    | mk v hv =>
      Hint (hidden := true) "Case-split on `v`:
      `cases v with | zero => ... | succ n => ...`"
      cases v with
      | zero =>
        Hint (hidden := true) "The element is `0`. Provide `1` as witness:
        `use Ō¤©1, by omegaŌ¤®`, then prove `0 ŌēĀ 1` with `intro h; cases h`."
        use Ō¤©1, by omegaŌ¤®
        intro h
        cases h
      | succ n =>
        Hint (hidden := true) "The element has value `n + 1`. Split on `n`."
        cases n with
        | zero =>
          Hint (hidden := true) "The element is `1`. Use `Ō¤©0, by omegaŌ¤®`
          as witness, then `intro h; cases h`."
          use Ō¤©0, by omegaŌ¤®
          intro h
          cases h
        | succ m =>
          Hint (hidden := true) "The element has value `m + 2`. Split on `m`."
          cases m with
          | zero =>
            Hint (hidden := true) "The element is `2`. Use `Ō¤©0, by omegaŌ¤®`
            as witness, then `intro h; cases h`."
            use Ō¤©0, by omegaŌ¤®
            intro h
            cases h
          | succ k =>
            Hint (hidden := true) "Here `v = k + 3` but `hv` says `v < 3`.
            This is impossible: `exact absurd hv (by omega)`."
            exact absurd hv (by omega)

Conclusion "
Congratulations ŌĆö you've completed **Meet Fin**!

You now have these core moves for working with `Fin n`:

| Move | Tool | Learned in |
|---|---|---|
| Construct a `Fin n` element | `Ō¤©k, by omegaŌ¤®` | Level 1 |
| Extract the number / coercion | `.val`, `.isLt`, `Ōåæi` | Level 2 |
| Upper bound proof | `.isLt` + `omega` | Level 3 |
| Combine bounds from multiple elements | multiple `.isLt` + `omega` | Level 4 |
| Prove `Fin` elements equal | `rw [Fin.ext_iff]` or `ext` | Levels 5, 15 |
| Rewrite hypotheses | `rw [...] at h` | Levels 7ŌĆō8 |
| Derive Fin.eta | `rw [Fin.ext_iff]; rfl` | Level 10 |
| Proof irrelevance | `Fin.ext_iff` + `omega` | Level 11 |
| General `Fin (n+1)` reasoning | `.isLt` + `omega` | Level 12 |
| Fin contradiction pattern | `.isLt` + `omega` on empty/impossible | Level 13 |
| Handle `Fin 1` (singleton) | `ext` + `omega` | Level 15 |
| Destructure and case-split | `cases` (nested) + `absurd` | Levels 16ŌĆō18 |
| Modular arithmetic | construction + `rfl` | Level 19 |
| Use Fin as a concrete index | case analysis on indexed data | Level 20 |
| Prove `Fin` inequality | `intro h; cases h` | Level 21 |
| Existential witness | `use Ō¤©k, by omegaŌ¤®` | Level 22 |

**Where this is going**: `Fin n` is the *canonical* finite type in
Lean ŌĆö the standard model that everything connects back to. Every
finite type with `n` elements is equivalent to `Fin n`, making it
the universal representative. A function `Fin n ŌåÆ ╬▒` is essentially
an `n`-tuple: an ordered list of `n` elements from `╬▒`. You'll
explore this idea in the FinTuples world.

Beyond tuples, you'll meet `Finset` (unordered finite collections),
`Fintype` (types with finitely many elements), and big operators
like `Ōłæ` and `ŌłÅ` over finite index sets. All of these are built on
the foundation you've just learned.

(As you saw in Level 19, `Fin n` also carries modular arithmetic ŌĆö
addition and multiplication wrap around mod `n`, making `Fin n` a
model of `Ōäż/nŌäż`. This course focuses on `Fin` as a finite index
type, not as a number system.)

In the next world, you'll learn to **navigate** within `Fin n` ŌĆö
moving forward with `Fin.succ`, embedding with `Fin.castSucc`, and
reaching the boundary with `Fin.last`.
"

   335ŌåÆWorld "FinNavigation"
   336ŌåÆLevel 5
   337ŌåÆ
   338ŌåÆTitle "castSucc is Below succ"
   339ŌåÆ
   340ŌåÆIntroduction "
   341ŌåÆ# Combining Val Lemmas
   342ŌåÆ
   343ŌåÆYou now know three val lemmas: `Fin.val_last`, `Fin.val_castSucc`,
   344ŌåÆand `Fin.val_succ`. Time to **combine** them.
   345ŌåÆ
   346ŌåÆIf `i : Fin n`, then `i.castSucc` and `i.succ` both live in
   347ŌåÆ`Fin (n+1)`. Which one is larger?
   348ŌåÆ
   349ŌåÆSince `castSucc` preserves the value and `succ` adds 1, clearly
   350ŌåÆ`castSucc` lands *below* `succ`. Let's prove this.
   351ŌåÆ
   352ŌåÆ**Strategy**: Rewrite both sides to values using their val lemmas,
   353ŌåÆthen let `omega` close the arithmetic.
   354ŌåÆ
   355ŌåÆ1. `intro i`
   356ŌåÆ2. `rw [Fin.val_castSucc, Fin.val_succ]` ŌĆö converts to `i.val < i.val + 1`
   357ŌåÆ3. `omega`
   358ŌåÆ"
   359ŌåÆ
   360ŌåÆ/-- `castSucc` always lands strictly below `succ`. -/
   361ŌåÆStatement : ŌłĆ i : Fin 3, i.castSucc.val < i.succ.val := by
   362ŌåÆ  Hint "Start with `intro i`."
   363ŌåÆ  intro i
   364ŌåÆ  Hint "Use `rw [Fin.val_castSucc, Fin.val_succ]` to convert both
   365ŌåÆ  sides to plain natural numbers."
   366ŌåÆ  rw [Fin.val_castSucc, Fin.val_succ]
   367ŌåÆ  Hint "The goal is `i.val < i.val + 1`. `omega` closes this."
   368ŌåÆ  omega
   369ŌåÆ
   370ŌåÆConclusion "
   371ŌåÆThe **rewrite-to-values** strategy works for comparing any two
   372ŌåÆ`Fin` expressions:
   373ŌåÆ1. Apply val lemmas to simplify `Fin` navigation to ŌäĢ arithmetic
   374ŌåÆ2. Use `omega` to close the resulting goal
   375ŌåÆ
   376ŌåÆThis is the standard recipe. It works because the ordering on
   377ŌåÆ`Fin n` is defined through values: `a < b` in `Fin` means
   378ŌåÆ`a.val < b.val` in `ŌäĢ`.
   379ŌåÆ
   380ŌåÆThe fact `i.castSucc.val < i.succ.val` means these two functions
   381ŌåÆnever agree ŌĆö they always land at different positions.
   382ŌåÆ`castSucc` fills positions $0, \\ldots, n-1$; `succ` fills
   383ŌåÆpositions $1, \\ldots, n$. They overlap on $1, \\ldots, n-1$
   384ŌåÆbut at different *input* values.
   385ŌåÆ"

   391ŌåÆWorld "FinNavigation"
   392ŌåÆLevel 6
   393ŌåÆ
   394ŌåÆTitle "castSucc and succ Never Agree"
   395ŌåÆ
   396ŌåÆIntroduction "
   397ŌåÆ# Different Embeddings, Different Outputs
   398ŌåÆ
   399ŌåÆIn Level 5, you proved that `i.castSucc.val < i.succ.val` ŌĆö the
   400ŌåÆvalue of `castSucc` is strictly less than the value of `succ`.
   401ŌåÆThe immediate consequence: **they can never be equal**.
   402ŌåÆ
   403ŌåÆIf `i.castSucc = i.succ`, then their values would be equal. But
   404ŌåÆyou just showed one is strictly less than the other. Contradiction.
   405ŌåÆ
   406ŌåÆThe proof uses the same value-reasoning pattern you've been
   407ŌåÆpracticing:
   408ŌåÆ1. `intro i h` ŌĆö assume `h : i.castSucc = i.succ`
   409ŌåÆ2. `rw [Fin.ext_iff] at h` ŌĆö convert to values
   410ŌåÆ3. `rw [Fin.val_castSucc, Fin.val_succ] at h` ŌĆö simplify
   411ŌåÆ4. `omega` ŌĆö `i.val = i.val + 1` is impossible
   412ŌåÆ
   413ŌåÆThis is a direct corollary of Level 5's strict inequality.
   414ŌåÆ"
   415ŌåÆ
   416ŌåÆ/-- `castSucc` and `succ` never produce the same output. -/
   417ŌåÆStatement : ŌłĆ i : Fin 3, i.castSucc ŌēĀ i.succ := by
   418ŌåÆ  Hint "Start with `intro i h` to assume they're equal."
   419ŌåÆ  intro i h
   420ŌåÆ  Hint "Convert to values: `rw [Fin.ext_iff] at h`."
   421ŌåÆ  rw [Fin.ext_iff] at h
   422ŌåÆ  Hint "Simplify both sides: `rw [Fin.val_castSucc, Fin.val_succ] at h`."
   423ŌåÆ  rw [Fin.val_castSucc, Fin.val_succ] at h
   424ŌåÆ  Hint "Now the hypothesis says `i.val = i.val + 1`. This is impossible
   425ŌåÆ  for natural numbers. `omega` closes the goal."
   426ŌåÆ  omega
   427ŌåÆ
   428ŌåÆConclusion "
   429ŌåÆThis completes the picture of how `castSucc` and `succ` relate:
   430ŌåÆ
   431ŌåÆ| Fact | Statement | Proved in |
   432ŌåÆ|---|---|---|
   433ŌåÆ| Value ordering | `i.castSucc.val < i.succ.val` | Level 5 |
   434ŌåÆ| Disjointness | `i.castSucc /= i.succ` | This level |
   435ŌåÆ
   436ŌåÆThe two embedding functions `castSucc` and `succ` have **disjoint
   437ŌåÆimages at each input** ŌĆö for any given `i`, the element `castSucc`
   438ŌåÆlands on is always different from where `succ` lands.
   439ŌåÆ
   440ŌåÆThis proof used a pattern that deserves a name: the **value
   441ŌåÆreduction strategy for hypotheses**. When you have a hypothesis
   442ŌåÆ`h` about `Fin` elements:
   443ŌåÆ1. `rw [Fin.ext_iff] at h` ŌĆö reduce to values
   444ŌåÆ2. `rw [val_lemma, ...] at h` ŌĆö simplify to ŌäĢ arithmetic
   445ŌåÆ3. `omega` ŌĆö derive a contradiction or close the goal
   446ŌåÆ
   447ŌåÆIn Level 5, you used the goal-level version (rewrite the *goal*
   448ŌåÆto values, then `omega`). Here you used the hypothesis-level
   449ŌåÆversion (rewrite the *hypothesis* to values, then `omega`). Both
   450ŌåÆare instances of the same idea: **reduce Fin reasoning to ŌäĢ
   451ŌåÆarithmetic, then let omega handle it**.
   452ŌåÆ
   453ŌåÆCombined with the separation facts (next levels) and the
   454ŌåÆdecompositions (later), this means `Fin (n+1)` is neatly
   455ŌåÆpartitioned by these two embedding functions.
   456ŌåÆ"
   457ŌåÆ

   462ŌåÆWorld "FinNavigation"
   463ŌåÆLevel 7
   464ŌåÆ
   465ŌåÆTitle "castSucc Never Reaches Last"
   466ŌåÆ
   467ŌåÆIntroduction "
   468ŌåÆ# The Key Structural Fact
   469ŌåÆ
   470ŌåÆHere is the most important fact about `Fin` navigation:
   471ŌåÆ
   472ŌåÆ> `castSucc` and `Fin.last` produce **disjoint** elements.
   473ŌåÆ
   474ŌåÆNo element in the image of `castSucc` can equal `Fin.last n`.
   475ŌåÆWhy? Because `castSucc` preserves values in $\\{0, \\ldots, n-1\\}$,
   476ŌåÆwhile `Fin.last n` has value $n$.
   477ŌåÆ
   478ŌåÆTo prove `j.castSucc ŌēĀ Fin.last 3`, you need to show that
   479ŌåÆassuming they are equal leads to a contradiction. The strategy:
   480ŌåÆ
   481ŌåÆ1. `intro j h` ŌĆö assume `h : j.castSucc = Fin.last 3`
   482ŌåÆ2. `rw [Fin.ext_iff] at h` ŌĆö convert to `j.castSucc.val = (Fin.last 3).val`
   483ŌåÆ3. `rw [Fin.val_castSucc, Fin.val_last] at h` ŌĆö simplify to `j.val = 3`
   484ŌåÆ4. `omega` ŌĆö contradicts `j.val < 3` (since `j : Fin 3`)
   485ŌåÆ
   486ŌåÆThis is the **value reasoning** pattern: convert `Fin` facts to
   487ŌåÆvalue facts, then let arithmetic close the gap.
   488ŌåÆ"
   489ŌåÆ
   490ŌåÆ/-- No `castSucc` image equals `Fin.last`. -/
   491ŌåÆStatement : ŌłĆ j : Fin 3, j.castSucc ŌēĀ Fin.last 3 := by
   492ŌåÆ  Hint "Start with `intro j h` to assume equality for contradiction."
   493ŌåÆ  intro j h
   494ŌåÆ  Hint "Convert to values: `rw [Fin.ext_iff] at h`."
   495ŌåÆ  rw [Fin.ext_iff] at h
   496ŌåÆ  Hint "Simplify: `rw [Fin.val_castSucc, Fin.val_last] at h`.
   497ŌåÆ  This gives `h : j.val = 3`."
   498ŌåÆ  rw [Fin.val_castSucc, Fin.val_last] at h
   499ŌåÆ  Hint "Now `{h}` says `j.val = 3`, but `j : Fin 3` means
   500ŌåÆ  `j.val < 3`. `omega` sees the contradiction."
   501ŌåÆ  omega
   502ŌåÆ
   503ŌåÆConclusion "
   504ŌåÆThis is the **separation fact**: the images of `castSucc`
   505ŌåÆ(values $0, \\ldots, n-1$) and `Fin.last` (value $n$) don't overlap.
   506ŌåÆ
   507ŌåÆTogether with the *maximum fact* from Level 2, this tells you:
   508ŌåÆ`Fin (n+1)` splits into two disjoint parts:
   509ŌåÆ- Elements with value $< n$ (the `castSucc` images)
   510ŌåÆ- The single element with value $= n$ (`Fin.last n`)
   511ŌåÆ
   512ŌåÆThis decomposition is like the natural number fact that every
   513ŌåÆ$k \\leq n$ either satisfies $k < n$ or $k = n$.
   514ŌåÆ
   515ŌåÆThe proof pattern ŌĆö `ext_iff`, then val lemmas, then `omega` ŌĆö
   516ŌåÆis how you reason about `Fin` equality and inequality at the
   517ŌåÆvalue level. You'll use it again in the boss.
   518ŌåÆ"
   519ŌåÆ
   520ŌåÆDisabledTactic trivial decide native_decide simp aesop simp_all fin_cases interval_cases norm_num

   522ŌåÆimport Game.Metadata
   523ŌåÆ
   524ŌåÆWorld "FinNavigation"
   525ŌåÆLevel 8
   526ŌåÆ
   527ŌåÆTitle "castSucc is Injective"
   528ŌåÆ
   529ŌåÆIntroduction "
   530ŌåÆ# castSucc Preserves Distinctness
   531ŌåÆ
   532ŌåÆIn Level 7, you proved that `castSucc` images are disjoint from
   533ŌåÆ`Fin.last`. But there's another important property: `castSucc` is
   534ŌåÆ**injective** ŌĆö it never sends two different elements to the same
   535ŌåÆplace.
   536ŌåÆ
   537ŌåÆWhy? Because `castSucc` preserves values. If
   538ŌåÆ`i.castSucc = j.castSucc`, then their values are equal
   539ŌåÆ(`i.castSucc.val = j.castSucc.val`), and since `castSucc` preserves
   540ŌåÆvalues (`val_castSucc`), we get `i.val = j.val`, so `i = j`.
   541ŌåÆ
   542ŌåÆThis matters because the decomposition (which you'll prove in the
   543ŌåÆboss) depends on `castSucc` being a genuine embedding ŌĆö mapping
   544ŌåÆ`Fin n` *injectively* into `Fin (n+1)`. If `castSucc` could collapse
   545ŌåÆtwo elements, the decomposition would be wrong.
   546ŌåÆ
   547ŌåÆ**Strategy**: The pattern is the same as succ injectivity:
   548ŌåÆ1. `intro i j h`
   549ŌåÆ2. `rw [Fin.ext_iff] at h` ŌĆö convert equality to values
   550ŌåÆ3. `rw [Fin.val_castSucc, Fin.val_castSucc] at h` ŌĆö simplify
   551ŌåÆ4. `ext` ŌĆö reduce the goal to values
   552ŌåÆ5. `exact h` ŌĆö close with the hypothesis
   553ŌåÆ"
   554ŌåÆ
   555ŌåÆ/-- `castSucc` is injective: equal outputs imply equal inputs. -/
   556ŌåÆStatement : ŌłĆ i j : Fin 3, i.castSucc = j.castSucc ŌåÆ i = j := by
   557ŌåÆ  Hint "Start with `intro i j h`."
   558ŌåÆ  intro i j h
   559ŌåÆ  Hint "Convert `{h}` to a value equality: `rw [Fin.ext_iff] at h`."
   560ŌåÆ  rw [Fin.ext_iff] at h
   561ŌåÆ  Hint "Simplify the `castSucc` values:
   562ŌåÆ  `rw [Fin.val_castSucc, Fin.val_castSucc] at h`.
   563ŌåÆ  This gives `{h}` as `i.val = j.val`."
   564ŌåÆ  rw [Fin.val_castSucc, Fin.val_castSucc] at h
   565ŌåÆ  Hint "Now reduce the goal to values with `ext`, then close with
   566ŌåÆ  `exact h`."
   567ŌåÆ  ext
   568ŌåÆ  exact h
   569ŌåÆ
   570ŌåÆConclusion "
   571ŌåÆThe **injectivity pattern** for `Fin` functions:
   572ŌåÆ```
   573ŌåÆrw [Fin.ext_iff] at h
   574ŌåÆrw [val_lemma, val_lemma] at h
   575ŌåÆext
   576ŌåÆexact h  -- or omega
   577ŌåÆ```
   578ŌåÆ
   579ŌåÆConvert the hypothesis to values, simplify with val lemmas, then

Fin.succ i  -- explicit
```

## Type
`Fin.succ : Fin n ŌåÆ Fin (n + 1)`

## Value
`i.succ.val = i.val + 1` (see `Fin.val_succ`)

## Comparison with castSucc
- `castSucc`: same value, bigger type
- `succ`: value + 1, bigger type
-/
DefinitionDoc Fin.succ as "Fin.succ"

/-- `Fin.val_succ i` states that `i.succ.val = i.val + 1`.

## Syntax
```
rw [Fin.val_succ]      -- rewrites i.succ.val to i.val + 1
exact Fin.val_succ i   -- closes the goal directly
```

## When to use it
When you need to simplify `Fin.succ` to the underlying value.
-/

import Game.Metadata

World "FinNavigation"
Level 5

Title "castSucc is Below succ"

Introduction "
# Combining Val Lemmas

You now know three val lemmas: `Fin.val_last`, `Fin.val_castSucc`,
and `Fin.val_succ`. Time to **combine** them.

If `i : Fin n`, then `i.castSucc` and `i.succ` both live in
`Fin (n+1)`. Which one is larger?

Since `castSucc` preserves the value and `succ` adds 1, clearly
`castSucc` lands *below* `succ`. Let's prove this.

**Strategy**: Rewrite both sides to values using their val lemmas,
then let `omega` close the arithmetic.

1. `intro i`
2. `rw [Fin.val_castSucc, Fin.val_succ]` ŌĆö converts to `i.val < i.val + 1`
3. `omega`
"

/-- `castSucc` always lands strictly below `succ`. -/
Statement : ŌłĆ i : Fin 3, i.castSucc.val < i.succ.val := by
  Hint "Start with `intro i`."
  intro i
  Hint "Use `rw [Fin.val_castSucc, Fin.val_succ]` to convert both
  sides to plain natural numbers."
  rw [Fin.val_castSucc, Fin.val_succ]
  Hint "The goal is `i.val < i.val + 1`. `omega` closes this."
  omega

Conclusion "
The **rewrite-to-values** strategy works for comparing any two
`Fin` expressions:
1. Apply val lemmas to simplify `Fin` navigation to ŌäĢ arithmetic
2. Use `omega` to close the resulting goal

This is the standard recipe. It works because the ordering on
`Fin n` is defined through values: `a < b` in `Fin` means
`a.val < b.val` in `ŌäĢ`.

The fact `i.castSucc.val < i.succ.val` means these two functions
never agree ŌĆö they always land at different positions.
`castSucc` fills positions $0, \\ldots, n-1$; `succ` fills
positions $1, \\ldots, n$. They overlap on $1, \\ldots, n-1$
but at different *input* values.
"

import Game.Metadata

World "FinNavigation"
Level 6

Title "castSucc and succ Never Agree"

Introduction "
# Different Embeddings, Different Outputs

In Level 5, you proved that `i.castSucc.val < i.succ.val` ŌĆö the
value of `castSucc` is strictly less than the value of `succ`.
The immediate consequence: **they can never be equal**.

If `i.castSucc = i.succ`, then their values would be equal. But
you just showed one is strictly less than the other. Contradiction.

The proof uses the same value-reasoning pattern you've been
practicing:
1. `intro i h` ŌĆö assume `h : i.castSucc = i.succ`
2. `rw [Fin.ext_iff] at h` ŌĆö convert to values
3. `rw [Fin.val_castSucc, Fin.val_succ] at h` ŌĆö simplify
4. `omega` ŌĆö `i.val = i.val + 1` is impossible

This is a direct corollary of Level 5's strict inequality.
"

/-- `castSucc` and `succ` never produce the same output. -/
Statement : ŌłĆ i : Fin 3, i.castSucc ŌēĀ i.succ := by
  Hint "Start with `intro i h` to assume they're equal."
  intro i h
  Hint "Convert to values: `rw [Fin.ext_iff] at h`."
  rw [Fin.ext_iff] at h
  Hint "Simplify both sides: `rw [Fin.val_castSucc, Fin.val_succ] at h`."
  rw [Fin.val_castSucc, Fin.val_succ] at h
  Hint "Now the hypothesis says `i.val = i.val + 1`. This is impossible
  for natural numbers. `omega` closes the goal."
  omega

Conclusion "
This completes the picture of how `castSucc` and `succ` relate:

| Fact | Statement | Proved in |
|---|---|---|
| Value ordering | `i.castSucc.val < i.succ.val` | Level 5 |
| Disjointness | `i.castSucc /= i.succ` | This level |

The two embedding functions `castSucc` and `succ` have **disjoint
images at each input** ŌĆö for any given `i`, the element `castSucc`
lands on is always different from where `succ` lands.

This proof used a pattern that deserves a name: the **value
reduction strategy for hypotheses**. When you have a hypothesis
`h` about `Fin` elements:
1. `rw [Fin.ext_iff] at h` ŌĆö reduce to values
2. `rw [val_lemma, ...] at h` ŌĆö simplify to ŌäĢ arithmetic
3. `omega` ŌĆö derive a contradiction or close the goal

In Level 5, you used the goal-level version (rewrite the *goal*
to values, then `omega`). Here you used the hypothesis-level
version (rewrite the *hypothesis* to values, then `omega`). Both
are instances of the same idea: **reduce Fin reasoning to ŌäĢ
arithmetic, then let omega handle it**.

Combined with the separation facts (next levels) and the
decompositions (later), this means `Fin (n+1)` is neatly
partitioned by these two embedding functions.
"

import Game.Metadata

World "FinNavigation"
Level 7

Title "castSucc Never Reaches Last"

Introduction "
# The Key Structural Fact

Here is the most important fact about `Fin` navigation:

> `castSucc` and `Fin.last` produce **disjoint** elements.

No element in the image of `castSucc` can equal `Fin.last n`.
Why? Because `castSucc` preserves values in $\\{0, \\ldots, n-1\\}$,
while `Fin.last n` has value $n$.

To prove `j.castSucc ŌēĀ Fin.last 3`, you need to show that
assuming they are equal leads to a contradiction. The strategy:

1. `intro j h` ŌĆö assume `h : j.castSucc = Fin.last 3`
2. `rw [Fin.ext_iff] at h` ŌĆö convert to `j.castSucc.val = (Fin.last 3).val`
3. `rw [Fin.val_castSucc, Fin.val_last] at h` ŌĆö simplify to `j.val = 3`
4. `omega` ŌĆö contradicts `j.val < 3` (since `j : Fin 3`)

This is the **value reasoning** pattern: convert `Fin` facts to
value facts, then let arithmetic close the gap.
"

/-- No `castSucc` image equals `Fin.last`. -/
Statement : ŌłĆ j : Fin 3, j.castSucc ŌēĀ Fin.last 3 := by
  Hint "Start with `intro j h` to assume equality for contradiction."
  intro j h
  Hint "Convert to values: `rw [Fin.ext_iff] at h`."
  rw [Fin.ext_iff] at h
  Hint "Simplify: `rw [Fin.val_castSucc, Fin.val_last] at h`.
  This gives `h : j.val = 3`."
  rw [Fin.val_castSucc, Fin.val_last] at h
  Hint "Now `{h}` says `j.val = 3`, but `j : Fin 3` means
  `j.val < 3`. `omega` sees the contradiction."
  omega

Conclusion "
This is the **separation fact**: the images of `castSucc`
(values $0, \\ldots, n-1$) and `Fin.last` (value $n$) don't overlap.

Together with the *maximum fact* from Level 2, this tells you:
`Fin (n+1)` splits into two disjoint parts:
- Elements with value $< n$ (the `castSucc` images)
- The single element with value $= n$ (`Fin.last n`)

This decomposition is like the natural number fact that every
$k \\leq n$ either satisfies $k < n$ or $k = n$.

The proof pattern ŌĆö `ext_iff`, then val lemmas, then `omega` ŌĆö
is how you reason about `Fin` equality and inequality at the
value level. You'll use it again in the boss.
"

World "FinNavigation"
Level 8

Title "castSucc is Injective"

Introduction "
# castSucc Preserves Distinctness

In Level 7, you proved that `castSucc` images are disjoint from
`Fin.last`. But there's another important property: `castSucc` is
**injective** ŌĆö it never sends two different elements to the same
place.

Why? Because `castSucc` preserves values. If
`i.castSucc = j.castSucc`, then their values are equal
(`i.castSucc.val = j.castSucc.val`), and since `castSucc` preserves
values (`val_castSucc`), we get `i.val = j.val`, so `i = j`.

This matters because the decomposition (which you'll prove in the
boss) depends on `castSucc` being a genuine embedding ŌĆö mapping
`Fin n` *injectively* into `Fin (n+1)`. If `castSucc` could collapse
two elements, the decomposition would be wrong.

**Strategy**: The pattern is the same as succ injectivity:
1. `intro i j h`
2. `rw [Fin.ext_iff] at h` ŌĆö convert equality to values
3. `rw [Fin.val_castSucc, Fin.val_castSucc] at h` ŌĆö simplify
4. `ext` ŌĆö reduce the goal to values
5. `exact h` ŌĆö close with the hypothesis
"

/-- `castSucc` is injective: equal outputs imply equal inputs. -/
Statement : ŌłĆ i j : Fin 3, i.castSucc = j.castSucc ŌåÆ i = j := by
  Hint "Start with `intro i j h`."
  intro i j h
  Hint "Convert `{h}` to a value equality: `rw [Fin.ext_iff] at h`."
  rw [Fin.ext_iff] at h
  Hint "Simplify the `castSucc` values:
  `rw [Fin.val_castSucc, Fin.val_castSucc] at h`.
  This gives `{h}` as `i.val = j.val`."
  rw [Fin.val_castSucc, Fin.val_castSucc] at h
  Hint "Now reduce the goal to values with `ext`, then close with
  `exact h`."
  ext
  exact h

Conclusion "
The **injectivity pattern** for `Fin` functions:
```
rw [Fin.ext_iff] at h
rw [val_lemma, val_lemma] at h
ext
exact h  -- or omega
```

Convert the hypothesis to values, simplify with val lemmas, then
transfer to the goal. This is exactly the same pattern you'll use
in Level 9 for `succ` injectivity ŌĆö the only difference is which
val lemma you use (`val_castSucc` vs `val_succ`).

Both `castSucc` and `succ` are injective ŌĆö you've just proved it
for `castSucc`, and you'll prove it for `succ` next. Together with
the separation fact (Level 7), this tells you: `castSucc` is a
*perfect copy* of `Fin n` inside `Fin (n+1)`, preserving both
values and distinctness.
"

World "FinNavigation"
Level 9

Title "succ is Injective"

Introduction "
# succ Preserves Distinctness

In Level 8, you proved that `castSucc` is injective. The same is
true for `succ`: if `i.succ = j.succ`, then `i = j`.

Why? Because `succ` adds 1 to the value. If
`i.succ.val = j.succ.val`, then `i.val + 1 = j.val + 1`, so
`i.val = j.val`, so `i = j`.

The proof pattern is identical to Level 8 ŌĆö just swap
`val_castSucc` for `val_succ`:
1. `intro i j h`
2. `rw [Fin.ext_iff] at h` ŌĆö convert equality to values
3. `rw [Fin.val_succ, Fin.val_succ] at h` ŌĆö simplify
4. `ext` ŌĆö reduce the goal to values
5. `omega` ŌĆö close with the simplified hypothesis
"

/-- `succ` is injective: equal outputs imply equal inputs. -/
Statement : ŌłĆ i j : Fin 3, i.succ = j.succ ŌåÆ i = j := by
  Hint "Start with `intro i j h`."
  intro i j h
  Hint "Convert `{h}` to a value equality: `rw [Fin.ext_iff] at h`."
  rw [Fin.ext_iff] at h
  Hint "Simplify the `succ` values:
  `rw [Fin.val_succ, Fin.val_succ] at h`.
  This gives `i.val + 1 = j.val + 1`."
  rw [Fin.val_succ, Fin.val_succ] at h
  Hint "Now reduce the goal to values with `ext`, then close with
  `omega`."
  ext
  omega

Conclusion "
The injectivity pattern works the same for both navigation functions:

| Function | Convert hypothesis | Simplify | Close |
|---|---|---|---|
| `castSucc` | `rw [Fin.ext_iff] at h` | `rw [val_castSucc, val_castSucc] at h` | `ext; exact h` |
| `succ` | `rw [Fin.ext_iff] at h` | `rw [val_succ, val_succ] at h` | `ext; omega` |

The only difference: after simplifying `succ` values, `h` says
`i.val + 1 = j.val + 1` instead of `i.val = j.val`, so you need
`omega` instead of `exact h` to cancel the `+ 1`.

Both `castSucc` and `succ` are injective ŌĆö each gives a *faithful*
copy of `Fin n` inside `Fin (n+1)`.
"

World "FinNavigation"
Level 10

Title "The Other Decomposition"

Introduction "
# The 0/succ Decomposition

In Levels 1-9, you studied the **castSucc/last** path:
separation (Level 7), injectivity (Level 8), and the dual
`succ` injectivity (Level 9). Now for the second decomposition.
every element of `Fin (n+1)` is either `Fin.last n` or
`j.castSucc` for some `j : Fin n`.

There is a **dual decomposition**: every element of `Fin (n+1)`
is either `0` or `j.succ` for some `j : Fin n`.

Why? An element `i : Fin (n+1)` has value `v` in
$\\{0, 1, \\ldots, n\\}$. If `v = 0`, then `i = 0`. If `v >= 1`,
then `v = (v-1) + 1`, so `i = j.succ` where
`j = Ō¤©v-1, ...Ō¤® : Fin n`.

These two decompositions are **dual**:

| Decomposition | Cases | Uses |
|---|---|---|
| castSucc/last | `i = j.castSucc` or `i = Fin.last n` | Appending (vecSnoc) |
| 0/succ | `i = 0` or `i = j.succ` | Prepending (Fin.cons) |

In the next world, you'll see how `Fin.cons` uses the 0/succ
decomposition: the head goes to index `0`, and the tail goes
to the `succ` indices.

**Your task**: Prove the 0/succ decomposition for `Fin 3`.
The strategy is the same case analysis pattern: destructure,
split on the value, handle each case.
"

/-- Every element of Fin 3 is either 0 or a successor. -/
Statement : ŌłĆ i : Fin 3, i = 0 Ōł© Ōłā j : Fin 2, i = j.succ := by
  Hint "Start with `intro i`, then destructure:
  `cases i with | mk v hv =>`."
  intro i
  Hint (hidden := true) "`cases i with | mk v hv =>`"
  cases i with
  | mk v hv =>

content.content:
700ŌåÆ    Hint "Now case-split on `v`: is the value `0` or `succ n`?"
   701ŌåÆ    Hint (hidden := true) "`cases v with | zero => ... | succ n => ...`"
   702ŌåÆ    cases v with
   703ŌåÆ    | zero =>
   704ŌåÆ      Hint "Here `v = 0`, so `i = 0`. Choose `left` and use `rfl`."
   705ŌåÆ      left
   706ŌåÆ      rfl
   707ŌåÆ    | succ n =>
   708ŌåÆ      Hint "Here `v = n + 1`, so `i = j.succ` where
   709ŌåÆ      `j = Ō¤©n, ...Ō¤® : Fin 2`. Choose `right`, provide the witness,
   710ŌåÆ      then prove the equality."
   711ŌåÆ      right
   712ŌåÆ      Hint (hidden := true) "`use Ō¤©n, by omegaŌ¤®`"
   713ŌåÆ      use Ō¤©n, by omegaŌ¤®
   714ŌåÆ      Hint (hidden := true) "Use `ext` to reduce to values,
   715ŌåÆ      then `rw [Fin.val_succ]`."
   716ŌåÆ      ext
   717ŌåÆ      rw [Fin.val_succ]
   718ŌåÆ
   719ŌåÆConclusion "
   720ŌåÆYou've proved the **0/succ decomposition**: every element of
   721ŌåÆ`Fin (n+1)` is either `0` (the minimum) or `j.succ` for some
   722ŌåÆ`j : Fin n` (a shifted element).
   723ŌåÆ
   724ŌåÆCombined with the **castSucc/last decomposition** from Levels 1-8,
   725ŌåÆyou now have two ways to partition `Fin (n+1)`:
   726ŌåÆ
   727ŌåÆ| Decomposition | Singleton | Image |
   728ŌåÆ|---|---|---|
   729ŌåÆ| castSucc/last | `Fin.last n` (value $n$) | `j.castSucc` (values $0, \\ldots, n-1$) |
   730ŌåÆ| 0/succ | `0` (value $0$) | `j.succ` (values $1, \\ldots, n$) |
   731ŌåÆ
   732ŌåÆThe duality is: castSucc/last splits off the *maximum*;
   733ŌåÆ0/succ splits off the *minimum*.
   734ŌåÆ
   735ŌåÆThis dual structure is the reason tuples can be built from either
   736ŌåÆend: `Fin.cons` routes through `0`/`succ`, and `vecSnoc` routes
   737ŌåÆthrough `castSucc`/`last`.
   738ŌåÆ"
   739ŌåÆ

   742ŌåÆimport Game.Metadata
   743ŌåÆ
   744ŌåÆWorld "FinNavigation"
   745ŌåÆLevel 11
   746ŌåÆ
   747ŌåÆTitle "succ Never Reaches Zero"
   748ŌåÆ
   749ŌåÆIntroduction "
   750ŌåÆ# The Dual Separation Fact
   751ŌåÆ
   752ŌåÆIn Level 7, you proved that `castSucc` images never equal
   753ŌåÆ`Fin.last` ŌĆö the **castSucc/last separation**.
   754ŌåÆ
   755ŌåÆHere is the dual: `succ` images never equal `0` ŌĆö the
   756ŌåÆ**0/succ separation**.
   757ŌåÆ
   758ŌåÆWhy? Because `i.succ.val = i.val + 1 >= 1`, but
   759ŌåÆ`(0 : Fin (n+1)).val = 0`. They can't be equal.
   760ŌåÆ
   761ŌåÆThe proof uses the same value-reasoning pattern:
   762ŌåÆ1. `intro i h` ŌĆö assume `h : i.succ = 0`
   763ŌåÆ2. `rw [Fin.ext_iff] at h` ŌĆö convert to values
   764ŌåÆ3. `rw [Fin.val_succ, Fin.val_zero] at h` ŌĆö simplify both sides
   765ŌåÆ4. `omega` ŌĆö `i.val + 1 = 0` is impossible for natural numbers
   766ŌåÆ
   767ŌåÆNotice the new val lemma: **`Fin.val_zero`** says
   768ŌåÆ`(0 : Fin (n+1)).val = 0`. Just as `Fin.val_last` simplifies
   769ŌåÆ`(Fin.last n).val` to `n`, `Fin.val_zero` simplifies `(0).val`
   770ŌåÆto `0`.
   771ŌåÆ"
   772ŌåÆ
   773ŌåÆ/-- No `succ` image equals `0`. -/
   774ŌåÆStatement : ŌłĆ i : Fin 3, i.succ ŌēĀ (0 : Fin 4) := by
   775ŌåÆ  Hint "The goal is `i.succ ŌēĀ 0`, which means `i.succ = 0 ŌåÆ False`.
   776ŌåÆ  Start with `intro i h`."
   777ŌåÆ  intro i h
   778ŌåÆ  Hint "Convert to values: `rw [Fin.ext_iff] at h`."
   779ŌåÆ  rw [Fin.ext_iff] at h
   780ŌåÆ  Hint "Simplify both sides: `rw [Fin.val_succ, Fin.val_zero] at h`.
   781ŌåÆ  `Fin.val_succ` simplifies the left; `Fin.val_zero` simplifies the right."
   782ŌåÆ  rw [Fin.val_succ, Fin.val_zero] at h
   783ŌåÆ  Hint "Now `{h}` says `i.val + 1 = 0`. But `i.val + 1 >= 1 > 0` for
   784ŌåÆ  any natural number. `omega` sees the contradiction."
   785ŌåÆ  omega
   786ŌåÆ
   787ŌåÆConclusion "
   788ŌåÆThe two separation facts are duals:
   789ŌåÆ
   790ŌåÆ| Separation | Statement | Why |
   791ŌåÆ|---|---|---|
   792ŌåÆ| castSucc/last | `j.castSucc ŌēĀ Fin.last n` | `j.val < n` vs `n` |
   793ŌåÆ| 0/succ | `j.succ ŌēĀ 0` | `j.val + 1 >= 1` vs `0` |
   794ŌåÆ
   795ŌåÆTogether with their respective decompositions, these give two
   796ŌåÆ**disjoint** partitions of `Fin (n+1)`:
   797ŌåÆ
   798ŌåÆ- `Fin (n+1) = image(castSucc) U {last}` (disjointly)
   799ŌåÆ- `Fin (n+1) = {0} U image(succ)` (disjointly)
   800ŌåÆ
   801ŌåÆThe proof pattern is identical for both: convert to values, apply
   802ŌåÆthe val lemma, let `omega` see the contradiction.
   803ŌåÆ"
   804ŌåÆ
   805ŌåÆ/-- `Fin.val_zero` states that `(0 : Fin (n + 1)).val = 0`.
   806ŌåÆ
   807ŌåÆ## Syntax
   808ŌåÆ```
   809ŌåÆrw [Fin.val_zero]      -- rewrites (0 : Fin n).val to 0
   810ŌåÆ```
   811ŌåÆ
   812ŌåÆ## When to use it
   813ŌåÆWhen you need to simplify the value of the zero element of `Fin`.
   814ŌåÆThis is the dual of `Fin.val_last`: `val_last` simplifies the
   815ŌåÆmaximum element's value, `val_zero` simplifies the minimum.
   816ŌåÆ-/
   817ŌåÆTheoremDoc Fin.val_zero as "Fin.val_zero" in "Fin"
   818ŌåÆ
   819ŌåÆNewTheorem Fin.val_zero
   820ŌåÆ

   826ŌåÆLevel 12
   827ŌåÆ
   828ŌåÆTitle "Recognizing the Last Element"
   829ŌåÆ
   830ŌåÆIntroduction "
   831ŌåÆ# When is an Element Fin.last?
   832ŌåÆ
   833ŌåÆIn Level 7, you proved that `castSucc` images can never equal
   834ŌåÆ`Fin.last`. The converse question: if you *know* an element's
   835ŌåÆvalue equals `n`, can you conclude it IS `Fin.last n`?
   836ŌåÆ
   837ŌåÆYes. Two `Fin` elements are equal when their values are equal
   838ŌåÆ(`ext` from the previous world). So if `i.val = 3` and
   839ŌåÆ`(Fin.last 3).val = 3`, then `i = Fin.last 3`.
   840ŌåÆ
   841ŌåÆ**Strategy**:
   842ŌåÆ1. `intro i h` ŌĆö introduce the element and hypothesis
   843ŌåÆ2. `ext` ŌĆö reduce `i = Fin.last 3` to `i.val = (Fin.last 3).val`
   844ŌåÆ3. `rw [Fin.val_last]` ŌĆö simplify RHS to `3`
   845ŌåÆ4. `exact h` ŌĆö the hypothesis gives `i.val = 3`
   846ŌåÆ"
   847ŌåÆ
   848ŌåÆ/-- An element with value `n` equals `Fin.last n`. -/
   849ŌåÆStatement : ŌłĆ i : Fin 4, i.val = 3 ŌåÆ i = Fin.last 3 := by
   850ŌåÆ  Hint "Start with `intro i h`."
   851ŌåÆ  intro i h
   852ŌåÆ  Hint "Use `ext` to reduce the equality to values."
   853ŌåÆ  ext
   854ŌåÆ  Hint "Use `rw [Fin.val_last]` to simplify `(Fin.last 3).val` to `3`."
   855ŌåÆ  rw [Fin.val_last]
   856ŌåÆ  Hint "The goal is `i.val = 3`, which is exactly `{h}`."
   857ŌåÆ  exact h
   858ŌåÆ
   859ŌåÆConclusion "
   860ŌåÆThe pattern for recognizing `Fin.last`:
   861ŌåÆ```
   862ŌåÆext; rw [Fin.val_last]; ...
   863ŌåÆ```
   864ŌåÆ
   865ŌåÆThis converts `i = Fin.last n` into `i.val = n`, which you can
   866ŌåÆclose with a hypothesis or `omega`.
   867ŌåÆ
   868ŌåÆIn the boss, you'll use this pattern inside a case analysis: when
   869ŌåÆthe case split gives you the maximum value, you'll identify the
   870ŌåÆelement as `Fin.last` using exactly this recipe.
   871ŌåÆ
   872ŌåÆThe mirror image ŌĆö recognizing a `castSucc` image ŌĆö is next.
   873ŌåÆ"
   874ŌåÆ

   879ŌåÆLevel 13
   880ŌåÆ
   881ŌåÆTitle "Finding a castSucc Preimage"
   882ŌåÆ
   883ŌåÆIntroduction "
   884ŌåÆ# When is an Element a castSucc Image?
   885ŌåÆ
   886ŌåÆIf `i : Fin 4` has `i.val < 3`, then `i` must be `j.castSucc`
   887ŌåÆfor some `j : Fin 3`. Why? Because `j.castSucc.val = j.val`,
   888ŌåÆso you can build the preimage `j` using the same value: take
   889ŌåÆ`j := Ō¤©i.val, hŌ¤® : Fin 3` (where `h : i.val < 3`).
   890ŌåÆ
   891ŌåÆThis requires a creative step: you must **construct** the
   892ŌåÆpreimage yourself. The witness is `Ō¤©i.val, hŌ¤®` ŌĆö the same
   893ŌåÆnumber, but viewed as living in the smaller type.
   894ŌåÆ
   895ŌåÆ**Strategy**:
   896ŌåÆ1. `intro i h`
   897ŌåÆ2. `use Ō¤©i.val, hŌ¤®` ŌĆö provide the preimage
   898ŌåÆ3. `ext` ŌĆö reduce to value equality
   899ŌåÆ4. `rw [Fin.val_castSucc]` ŌĆö simplify `castSucc` value
   900ŌåÆ"
   901ŌåÆ
   902ŌåÆ/-- An element with value below `n` is a `castSucc` image. -/
   903ŌåÆStatement : ŌłĆ i : Fin 4, i.val < 3 ŌåÆ Ōłā j : Fin 3, i = j.castSucc := by
   904ŌåÆ  Hint "Start with `intro i h`."
   905ŌåÆ  intro i h
   906ŌåÆ  Hint "The preimage is `Ō¤©i.val, hŌ¤® : Fin 3` ŌĆö the same number
   907ŌåÆ  in the smaller type. Use `use Ō¤©i.val, hŌ¤®`."
   908ŌåÆ  use Ō¤©i.val, hŌ¤®
   909ŌåÆ  Hint "Now prove `i = (Ō¤©i.val, hŌ¤®).castSucc`. Use `ext` to
   910ŌåÆ  reduce to values."
   911ŌåÆ  ext
   912ŌåÆ  Hint "Use `rw [Fin.val_castSucc]` to simplify `castSucc.val`
   913ŌåÆ  to `i.val`."
   914ŌåÆ  rw [Fin.val_castSucc]
   915ŌåÆ
   916ŌåÆConclusion "
   917ŌåÆThe pattern for finding a `castSucc` preimage:
   918ŌåÆ```
   919ŌåÆuse Ō¤©i.val, hŌ¤®; ext; rw [Fin.val_castSucc]
   920ŌåÆ```
   921ŌåÆ
   922ŌåÆThe key insight: if you know `i.val < n`, then `Ō¤©i.val, hŌ¤®`
   923ŌåÆis a valid element of `Fin n`, and its `castSucc` has the same
   924ŌåÆvalue as `i`. Since two `Fin` elements with the same value are
   925ŌåÆequal (by `ext`), you're done.
   926ŌåÆ
   927ŌåÆThis proof move deserves a name: **value repackaging**. You take
   928ŌåÆthe same underlying value but justify it in a *different type* by
   929ŌåÆproviding a new bound proof. The pattern is always:
   930ŌåÆ`Ō¤©i.val, new_bound_proofŌ¤®`. You'll see value repackaging again
   931ŌåÆin the succ direction (building `Ō¤©v - 1, ...Ō¤® : Fin n` from
   932ŌåÆ`Ō¤©v, ...Ō¤® : Fin (n + 1)`) and in the problem set when proving
   933ŌåÆgeneral decomposition results. In the boss, you'll use this move
   934ŌåÆinside a case analysis, where the bound comes from the case split.
   935ŌåÆ"
   936ŌåÆ

   940ŌåÆWorld "FinNavigation"
   941ŌåÆLevel 14
   942ŌåÆ
   943ŌåÆTitle "castSucc Surjectivity"
   944ŌåÆ
   945ŌåÆIntroduction "
   946ŌåÆ# Every Non-Last Element is a castSucc Image
   947ŌåÆ
   948ŌåÆIn Level 7, you proved that no `castSucc` image equals `Fin.last`.
   949ŌåÆThis level proves the **converse**: if an element is NOT `Fin.last`,
   950ŌåÆthen it IS a `castSucc` image. Together, they give an exact
   951ŌåÆcharacterization: *an element of `Fin (n+1)` is a `castSucc`
   952ŌåÆimage if and only if it is not `Fin.last n`*.
   953ŌåÆ
   954ŌåÆThis completes the embedding picture for `castSucc`:
   955ŌåÆ- **Injectivity** (Level 8): different inputs give different outputs
   956ŌåÆ- **Separation** (Level 7): no output equals `Fin.last`
   957ŌåÆ- **Surjectivity** (this level): every non-`last` element IS an output (converse of L7)
   958ŌåÆ
   959ŌåÆTogether: `castSucc` is a *bijection* between `Fin n` and
   960ŌåÆ`Fin (n+1) minus the last element`.
   961ŌåÆ
   962ŌåÆ**Strategy**:
   963ŌåÆ1. Derive `i.val /= n` from `i /= Fin.last n`
   964ŌåÆ2. Use `omega` to get `i.val < n` (from `i.val < n + 1` and `i.val /= n`)
   965ŌåÆ3. Provide the witness `(i.val, proof)` and prove equality via `ext`
   966ŌåÆ"
   967ŌåÆ
   968ŌåÆ/-- Every element not equal to `Fin.last` is a `castSucc` image. -/
   969ŌåÆStatement (n : ŌäĢ) : ŌłĆ i : Fin (n + 1), i ŌēĀ Fin.last n ŌåÆ Ōłā j : Fin n, i = j.castSucc := by
   970ŌåÆ  Hint "Start with `intro i hne`."
   971ŌåÆ  intro i hne
   972ŌåÆ  Hint "First derive that `i.val /= n`. Use a `have` block:
   973ŌåÆ  assume `i.val = n`, then show `i = Fin.last n` (contradicting `hne`)."
   974ŌåÆ  Hint (hidden := true) "`have hval : i.val /= n := by`
   975ŌåÆ  then inside: `intro heq; apply hne; ext; rw [Fin.val_last]; exact heq`"
   976ŌåÆ  have hval : i.val ŌēĀ n := by
   977ŌåÆ    intro heq
   978ŌåÆ    apply hne
   979ŌåÆ    ext
   980ŌåÆ    rw [Fin.val_last]
   981ŌåÆ    exact heq
   982ŌåÆ  Hint "Now `i.val < n + 1` (from `i.isLt`) and `i.val /= n`
   983ŌåÆ  together give `i.val < n`. Provide the witness."
   984ŌåÆ  Hint (hidden := true) "`use Ō¤©i.val, by omegaŌ¤®; ext; rw [Fin.val_castSucc]`"
   985ŌåÆ  use Ō¤©i.val, by omegaŌ¤®
   986ŌåÆ  ext
   987ŌåÆ  rw [Fin.val_castSucc]
   988ŌåÆ
   989ŌåÆConclusion "
   990ŌåÆThe surjectivity proof introduced a new technique: **deriving a
   991ŌåÆvalue-level fact from a Fin-level hypothesis**. You turned
   992ŌåÆ`i /= Fin.last n` into `i.val /= n` by showing the contrapositive:
   993ŌåÆif the values were equal, `ext` would force the Fin elements to be
   994ŌåÆequal, contradicting the hypothesis.
   995ŌåÆ
   996ŌåÆThis level and Level 7 together give the **if-and-only-if**:
   997ŌåÆ
   998ŌåÆ> `i` is a `castSucc` image **iff** `i /= Fin.last n`
   999ŌåÆ
  1000ŌåÆLevel 7 proved the forward direction (castSucc images are never
  1001ŌåÆlast). This level proved the converse (non-last elements are always
  1002ŌåÆcastSucc images). Asking 'is the converse true?' is a fundamental
  1003ŌåÆmathematical habit ŌĆö and here the answer is yes.
  1004ŌåÆ
  1005ŌåÆWith injectivity, separation, and surjectivity, the castSucc/last
  1006ŌåÆdecomposition is now fully justified:
  1007ŌåÆ- **Exhaustion**: every `i` is either `last` or `j.castSucc` (boss)
  1008ŌåÆ- **Disjointness**: no `castSucc` image equals `last` (Level 7)
  1009ŌåÆ- **Completeness**: every non-`last` element IS a `castSucc` image (this level)
  1010ŌåÆ
  1011ŌåÆIn algebraic language, `castSucc` is an isomorphism between `Fin n`
  1012ŌåÆand the complement of the last element in `Fin (n+1)`.
  1013ŌåÆ"
  1014ŌåÆ

  1020ŌåÆLevel 15
  1021ŌåÆ
  1022ŌåÆTitle "Boss: The Decomposition"
  1023ŌåÆ
  1024ŌåÆIntroduction "
  1025ŌåÆ# Boss: Putting Navigation Together
  1026ŌåÆ
  1027ŌåÆTime to combine everything. This boss has two parts.
  1028ŌåÆ
  1029ŌåÆ**Part 1 ŌĆö The Decomposition**: Every element of `Fin 3` is
  1030ŌåÆeither `Fin.last 2` or `j.castSucc` for some `j : Fin 2`.
  1031ŌåÆ
  1032ŌåÆThis is the structural heart of `Fin` navigation: `Fin (n+1)`
  1033ŌåÆsplits into the `castSucc` images (from `Fin n`) and
  1034ŌåÆ`Fin.last n`. No element falls outside these two cases.
  1035ŌåÆ
  1036ŌåÆStrategy: destructure (Level 16 of MeetFin), case-split on
  1037ŌåÆthe value, then use Level 12 (recognizing last) or Level 13
  1038ŌåÆ(finding castSucc) for each case.
  1039ŌåÆ
  1040ŌåÆ**Part 2 ŌĆö Succ injectivity**: You proved this in Level 9.
  1041ŌåÆHere you combine it with the decomposition in a single proof.
  1042ŌåÆThe pattern is the same: `ext_iff`, `val_succ`, `ext`, `omega`.
  1043ŌåÆ"
  1044ŌåÆ
  1045ŌåÆ/-- The last-or-castSucc decomposition, and succ injectivity. -/
  1046ŌåÆStatement : (ŌłĆ i : Fin 3, i = Fin.last 2 Ōł© Ōłā j : Fin 2, i = j.castSucc) Ōł¦
  1047ŌåÆ            (ŌłĆ i j : Fin 3, i.succ = j.succ ŌåÆ i = j) := by
  1048ŌåÆ  Hint "The goal is a conjunction. Use `constructor` to split it."
  1049ŌåÆ  constructor
  1050ŌåÆ  -- Part 1: decomposition
  1051ŌåÆ  ┬Ę Hint "Part 1: prove every `Fin 3` element is `last` or `castSucc`.
  1052ŌåÆ    Start with `intro i`, then destructure `i` with
  1053ŌåÆ    `cases i with | mk v hv =>`."
  1054ŌåÆ    intro i
  1055ŌåÆ    Hint (hidden := true) "Destructure: `cases i with | mk v hv =>`"
  1056ŌåÆ    cases i with
  1057ŌåÆ    | mk v hv =>
  1058ŌåÆ      Hint "Now `v : ŌäĢ` and `hv : v < 3`. Case-split on `v`:
  1059ŌåÆ      `cases v with | zero => ... | succ n => ...`"
  1060ŌåÆ      cases v with
  1061ŌåÆ      | zero =>
  1062ŌåÆ        Hint "Here `v = 0`. This is a `castSucc` image (value `< 2`).
  1063ŌåÆ        Choose `right`, then provide the preimage `Ō¤©0, by omegaŌ¤® : Fin 2`,
  1064ŌåÆ        then `ext; rw [Fin.val_castSucc]`."
  1065ŌåÆ        right
  1066ŌåÆ        Hint "To provide the preimage, use `use Ō¤©i.val, proofŌ¤®` where
  1067ŌåÆ        `i.val` is the value and the proof shows it's less than 2.
  1068ŌåÆ        Here: `use Ō¤©0, by omegaŌ¤®`."
  1069ŌåÆ        Hint (hidden := true) "`use Ō¤©0, by omegaŌ¤®`"
  1070ŌåÆ        use Ō¤©0, by omegaŌ¤®
  1071ŌåÆ        Hint (hidden := true) "`ext; rw [Fin.val_castSucc]`"
  1072ŌåÆ        ext
  1073ŌåÆ        rw [Fin.val_castSucc]
  1074ŌåÆ      | succ n =>
  1075ŌåÆ        Hint (hidden := true) "Case-split on `n`:
  1076ŌåÆ        `cases n with | zero => ... | succ m => ...`"
  1077ŌåÆ        cases n with
  1078ŌåÆ        | zero =>
  1079ŌåÆ          Hint (hidden := true) "Here `v = 1`. Still a `castSucc` image.
  1080ŌåÆ          `right; use Ō¤©1, by omegaŌ¤®; ext; rw [Fin.val_castSucc]`"
  1081ŌåÆ          right
  1082ŌåÆ          use Ō¤©1, by omegaŌ¤®
  1083ŌåÆ          ext
  1084ŌåÆ          rw [Fin.val_castSucc]
  1085ŌåÆ        | succ m =>
  1086ŌåÆ          Hint (hidden := true) "Split on `m` once more."
  1087ŌåÆ          cases m with
  1088ŌåÆ          | zero =>
  1089ŌåÆ            Hint (hidden := true) "Here `v = 2 = Fin.last 2`. Choose `left`,
  1090ŌåÆ            then `ext; rw [Fin.val_last]`."
  1091ŌåÆ            left
  1092ŌåÆ            ext
  1093ŌåÆ            rw [Fin.val_last]
  1094ŌåÆ          | succ k =>
  1095ŌåÆ            Hint (hidden := true) "Here `v = k + 3` but `hv` says `v < 3`.
  1096ŌåÆ            Impossible: `exact absurd hv (by omega)`."
  1097ŌåÆ            exact absurd hv (by omega)
  1098ŌåÆ  -- Part 2: succ injectivity
  1099ŌåÆ  ┬Ę Hint "Part 2: prove succ is injective. Start with `intro i j h`."

file.content:
Hint "Now case-split on `v`: is the value `0` or `succ n`?"
    Hint (hidden := true) "`cases v with | zero => ... | succ n => ...`"
    cases v with
    | zero =>
      Hint "Here `v = 0`, so `i = 0`. Choose `left` and use `rfl`."
      left
      rfl
    | succ n =>
      Hint "Here `v = n + 1`, so `i = j.succ` where
      `j = Ō¤©n, ...Ō¤® : Fin 2`. Choose `right`, provide the witness,
      then prove the equality."
      right
      Hint (hidden := true) "`use Ō¤©n, by omegaŌ¤®`"
      use Ō¤©n, by omegaŌ¤®
      Hint (hidden := true) "Use `ext` to reduce to values,
      then `rw [Fin.val_succ]`."
      ext
      rw [Fin.val_succ]

Conclusion "
You've proved the **0/succ decomposition**: every element of
`Fin (n+1)` is either `0` (the minimum) or `j.succ` for some
`j : Fin n` (a shifted element).

Combined with the **castSucc/last decomposition** from Levels 1-8,
you now have two ways to partition `Fin (n+1)`:

| Decomposition | Singleton | Image |
|---|---|---|
| castSucc/last | `Fin.last n` (value $n$) | `j.castSucc` (values $0, \\ldots, n-1$) |
| 0/succ | `0` (value $0$) | `j.succ` (values $1, \\ldots, n$) |

The duality is: castSucc/last splits off the *maximum*;
0/succ splits off the *minimum*.

This dual structure is the reason tuples can be built from either
end: `Fin.cons` routes through `0`/`succ`, and `vecSnoc` routes
through `castSucc`/`last`.
"

import Game.Metadata

World "FinNavigation"
Level 11

Title "succ Never Reaches Zero"

Introduction "
# The Dual Separation Fact

In Level 7, you proved that `castSucc` images never equal
`Fin.last` ŌĆö the **castSucc/last separation**.

Here is the dual: `succ` images never equal `0` ŌĆö the
**0/succ separation**.

Why? Because `i.succ.val = i.val + 1 >= 1`, but
`(0 : Fin (n+1)).val = 0`. They can't be equal.

The proof uses the same value-reasoning pattern:
1. `intro i h` ŌĆö assume `h : i.succ = 0`
2. `rw [Fin.ext_iff] at h` ŌĆö convert to values
3. `rw [Fin.val_succ, Fin.val_zero] at h` ŌĆö simplify both sides
4. `omega` ŌĆö `i.val + 1 = 0` is impossible for natural numbers

Notice the new val lemma: **`Fin.val_zero`** says
`(0 : Fin (n+1)).val = 0`. Just as `Fin.val_last` simplifies
`(Fin.last n).val` to `n`, `Fin.val_zero` simplifies `(0).val`
to `0`.
"

/-- No `succ` image equals `0`. -/
Statement : ŌłĆ i : Fin 3, i.succ ŌēĀ (0 : Fin 4) := by
  Hint "The goal is `i.succ ŌēĀ 0`, which means `i.succ = 0 ŌåÆ False`.
  Start with `intro i h`."
  intro i h
  Hint "Convert to values: `rw [Fin.ext_iff] at h`."
  rw [Fin.ext_iff] at h
  Hint "Simplify both sides: `rw [Fin.val_succ, Fin.val_zero] at h`.
  `Fin.val_succ` simplifies the left; `Fin.val_zero` simplifies the right."
  rw [Fin.val_succ, Fin.val_zero] at h
  Hint "Now `{h}` says `i.val + 1 = 0`. But `i.val + 1 >= 1 > 0` for
  any natural number. `omega` sees the contradiction."
  omega

Conclusion "
The two separation facts are duals:

| Separation | Statement | Why |
|---|---|---|
| castSucc/last | `j.castSucc ŌēĀ Fin.last n` | `j.val < n` vs `n` |
| 0/succ | `j.succ ŌēĀ 0` | `j.val + 1 >= 1` vs `0` |

Together with their respective decompositions, these give two
**disjoint** partitions of `Fin (n+1)`:

- `Fin (n+1) = image(castSucc) U {last}` (disjointly)
- `Fin (n+1) = {0} U image(succ)` (disjointly)

The proof pattern is identical for both: convert to values, apply
the val lemma, let `omega` see the contradiction.
"

/-- `Fin.val_zero` states that `(0 : Fin (n + 1)).val = 0`.

## Syntax
```
rw [Fin.val_zero]      -- rewrites (0 : Fin n).val to 0
```

## When to use it
When you need to simplify the value of the zero element of `Fin`.
This is the dual of `Fin.val_last`: `val_last` simplifies the
maximum element's value, `val_zero` simplifies the minimum.
-/
TheoremDoc Fin.val_zero as "Fin.val_zero" in "Fin"

NewTheorem Fin.val_zero

World "FinNavigation"
Level 12

Title "Recognizing the Last Element"

Introduction "
# When is an Element Fin.last?

In Level 7, you proved that `castSucc` images can never equal
`Fin.last`. The converse question: if you *know* an element's
value equals `n`, can you conclude it IS `Fin.last n`?

Yes. Two `Fin` elements are equal when their values are equal
(`ext` from the previous world). So if `i.val = 3` and
`(Fin.last 3).val = 3`, then `i = Fin.last 3`.

**Strategy**:
1. `intro i h` ŌĆö introduce the element and hypothesis
2. `ext` ŌĆö reduce `i = Fin.last 3` to `i.val = (Fin.last 3).val`
3. `rw [Fin.val_last]` ŌĆö simplify RHS to `3`
4. `exact h` ŌĆö the hypothesis gives `i.val = 3`
"

/-- An element with value `n` equals `Fin.last n`. -/
Statement : ŌłĆ i : Fin 4, i.val = 3 ŌåÆ i = Fin.last 3 := by
  Hint "Start with `intro i h`."
  intro i h
  Hint "Use `ext` to reduce the equality to values."
  ext
  Hint "Use `rw [Fin.val_last]` to simplify `(Fin.last 3).val` to `3`."
  rw [Fin.val_last]
  Hint "The goal is `i.val = 3`, which is exactly `{h}`."
  exact h

Conclusion "
The pattern for recognizing `Fin.last`:
```
ext; rw [Fin.val_last]; ...
```

This converts `i = Fin.last n` into `i.val = n`, which you can
close with a hypothesis or `omega`.

In the boss, you'll use this pattern inside a case analysis: when
the case split gives you the maximum value, you'll identify the
element as `Fin.last` using exactly this recipe.

The mirror image ŌĆö recognizing a `castSucc` image ŌĆö is next.
"

World "FinNavigation"
Level 13

Title "Finding a castSucc Preimage"

Introduction "
# When is an Element a castSucc Image?

If `i : Fin 4` has `i.val < 3`, then `i` must be `j.castSucc`
for some `j : Fin 3`. Why? Because `j.castSucc.val = j.val`,
so you can build the preimage `j` using the same value: take
`j := Ō¤©i.val, hŌ¤® : Fin 3` (where `h : i.val < 3`).

This requires a creative step: you must **construct** the
preimage yourself. The witness is `Ō¤©i.val, hŌ¤®` ŌĆö the same
number, but viewed as living in the smaller type.

**Strategy**:
1. `intro i h`
2. `use Ō¤©i.val, hŌ¤®` ŌĆö provide the preimage
3. `ext` ŌĆö reduce to value equality
4. `rw [Fin.val_castSucc]` ŌĆö simplify `castSucc` value
"

/-- An element with value below `n` is a `castSucc` image. -/
Statement : ŌłĆ i : Fin 4, i.val < 3 ŌåÆ Ōłā j : Fin 3, i = j.castSucc := by
  Hint "Start with `intro i h`."
  intro i h
  Hint "The preimage is `Ō¤©i.val, hŌ¤® : Fin 3` ŌĆö the same number
  in the smaller type. Use `use Ō¤©i.val, hŌ¤®`."
  use Ō¤©i.val, hŌ¤®
  Hint "Now prove `i = (Ō¤©i.val, hŌ¤®).castSucc`. Use `ext` to
  reduce to values."
  ext
  Hint "Use `rw [Fin.val_castSucc]` to simplify `castSucc.val`
  to `i.val`."
  rw [Fin.val_castSucc]

Conclusion "
The pattern for finding a `castSucc` preimage:
```
use Ō¤©i.val, hŌ¤®; ext; rw [Fin.val_castSucc]
```

The key insight: if you know `i.val < n`, then `Ō¤©i.val, hŌ¤®`
is a valid element of `Fin n`, and its `castSucc` has the same
value as `i`. Since two `Fin` elements with the same value are
equal (by `ext`), you're done.

This proof move deserves a name: **value repackaging**. You take
the same underlying value but justify it in a *different type* by
providing a new bound proof. The pattern is always:
`Ō¤©i.val, new_bound_proofŌ¤®`. You'll see value repackaging again
in the succ direction (building `Ō¤©v - 1, ...Ō¤® : Fin n` from
`Ō¤©v, ...Ō¤® : Fin (n + 1)`) and in the problem set when proving
general decomposition results. In the boss, you'll use this move
inside a case analysis, where the bound comes from the case split.
"

import Game.Metadata

World "FinNavigation"
Level 14

Title "castSucc Surjectivity"

Introduction "
# Every Non-Last Element is a castSucc Image

In Level 7, you proved that no `castSucc` image equals `Fin.last`.
This level proves the **converse**: if an element is NOT `Fin.last`,
then it IS a `castSucc` image. Together, they give an exact
characterization: *an element of `Fin (n+1)` is a `castSucc`
image if and only if it is not `Fin.last n`*.

This completes the embedding picture for `castSucc`:
- **Injectivity** (Level 8): different inputs give different outputs
- **Separation** (Level 7): no output equals `Fin.last`
- **Surjectivity** (this level): every non-`last` element IS an output (converse of L7)

Together: `castSucc` is a *bijection* between `Fin n` and
`Fin (n+1) minus the last element`.

**Strategy**:
1. Derive `i.val /= n` from `i /= Fin.last n`
2. Use `omega` to get `i.val < n` (from `i.val < n + 1` and `i.val /= n`)
3. Provide the witness `(i.val, proof)` and prove equality via `ext`
"

/-- Every element not equal to `Fin.last` is a `castSucc` image. -/
Statement (n : ŌäĢ) : ŌłĆ i : Fin (n + 1), i ŌēĀ Fin.last n ŌåÆ Ōłā j : Fin n, i = j.castSucc := by
  Hint "Start with `intro i hne`."
  intro i hne
  Hint "First derive that `i.val /= n`. Use a `have` block:
  assume `i.val = n`, then show `i = Fin.last n` (contradicting `hne`)."
  Hint (hidden := true) "`have hval : i.val /= n := by`
  then inside: `intro heq; apply hne; ext; rw [Fin.val_last]; exact heq`"
  have hval : i.val ŌēĀ n := by
    intro heq
    apply hne
    ext
    rw [Fin.val_last]
    exact heq
  Hint "Now `i.val < n + 1` (from `i.isLt`) and `i.val /= n`
  together give `i.val < n`. Provide the witness."
  Hint (hidden := true) "`use Ō¤©i.val, by omegaŌ¤®; ext; rw [Fin.val_castSucc]`"
  use Ō¤©i.val, by omegaŌ¤®
  ext
  rw [Fin.val_castSucc]

Conclusion "
The surjectivity proof introduced a new technique: **deriving a
value-level fact from a Fin-level hypothesis**. You turned
`i /= Fin.last n` into `i.val /= n` by showing the contrapositive:
if the values were equal, `ext` would force the Fin elements to be
equal, contradicting the hypothesis.

This level and Level 7 together give the **if-and-only-if**:

> `i` is a `castSucc` image **iff** `i /= Fin.last n`

Level 7 proved the forward direction (castSucc images are never
last). This level proved the converse (non-last elements are always
castSucc images). Asking 'is the converse true?' is a fundamental
mathematical habit ŌĆö and here the answer is yes.

With injectivity, separation, and surjectivity, the castSucc/last
decomposition is now fully justified:
- **Exhaustion**: every `i` is either `last` or `j.castSucc` (boss)
- **Disjointness**: no `castSucc` image equals `last` (Level 7)
- **Completeness**: every non-`last` element IS a `castSucc` image (this level)

In algebraic language, `castSucc` is an isomorphism between `Fin n`
and the complement of the last element in `Fin (n+1)`.
"

World "FinNavigation"
Level 15

Title "Boss: The Decomposition"

Introduction "
# Boss: Putting Navigation Together

Time to combine everything. This boss has two parts.

**Part 1 ŌĆö The Decomposition**: Every element of `Fin 3` is
either `Fin.last 2` or `j.castSucc` for some `j : Fin 2`.

This is the structural heart of `Fin` navigation: `Fin (n+1)`
splits into the `castSucc` images (from `Fin n`) and
`Fin.last n`. No element falls outside these two cases.

Strategy: destructure (Level 16 of MeetFin), case-split on
the value, then use Level 12 (recognizing last) or Level 13
(finding castSucc) for each case.

**Part 2 ŌĆö Succ injectivity**: You proved this in Level 9.
Here you combine it with the decomposition in a single proof.
The pattern is the same: `ext_iff`, `val_succ`, `ext`, `omega`.
"

/-- The last-or-castSucc decomposition, and succ injectivity. -/
Statement : (ŌłĆ i : Fin 3, i = Fin.last 2 Ōł© Ōłā j : Fin 2, i = j.castSucc) Ōł¦
            (ŌłĆ i j : Fin 3, i.succ = j.succ ŌåÆ i = j) := by
  Hint "The goal is a conjunction. Use `constructor` to split it."
  constructor
  -- Part 1: decomposition
  ┬Ę Hint "Part 1: prove every `Fin 3` element is `last` or `castSucc`.
    Start with `intro i`, then destructure `i` with
    `cases i with | mk v hv =>`."
    intro i
    Hint (hidden := true) "Destructure: `cases i with | mk v hv =>`"
    cases i with
    | mk v hv =>
      Hint "Now `v : ŌäĢ` and `hv : v < 3`. Case-split on `v`:
      `cases v with | zero => ... | succ n => ...`"
      cases v with
      | zero =>
        Hint "Here `v = 0`. This is a `castSucc` image (value `< 2`).
        Choose `right`, then provide the preimage `Ō¤©0, by omegaŌ¤® : Fin 2`,
        then `ext; rw [Fin.val_castSucc]`."
        right
        Hint "To provide the preimage, use `use Ō¤©i.val, proofŌ¤®` where
        `i.val` is the value and the proof shows it's less than 2.
        Here: `use Ō¤©0, by omegaŌ¤®`."
        Hint (hidden := true) "`use Ō¤©0, by omegaŌ¤®`"
        use Ō¤©0, by omegaŌ¤®
        Hint (hidden := true) "`ext; rw [Fin.val_castSucc]`"
        ext
        rw [Fin.val_castSucc]
      | succ n =>
        Hint (hidden := true) "Case-split on `n`:
        `cases n with | zero => ... | succ m => ...`"
        cases n with
        | zero =>
          Hint (hidden := true) "Here `v = 1`. Still a `castSucc` image.
          `right; use Ō¤©1, by omegaŌ¤®; ext; rw [Fin.val_castSucc]`"
          right
          use Ō¤©1, by omegaŌ¤®
          ext
          rw [Fin.val_castSucc]
        | succ m =>
          Hint (hidden := true) "Split on `m` once more."
          cases m with
          | zero =>
            Hint (hidden := true) "Here `v = 2 = Fin.last 2`. Choose `left`,
            then `ext; rw [Fin.val_last]`."
            left
            ext
            rw [Fin.val_last]
          | succ k =>
            Hint (hidden := true) "Here `v = k + 3` but `hv` says `v < 3`.
            Impossible: `exact absurd hv (by omega)`."
            exact absurd hv (by omega)
  -- Part 2: succ injectivity
  ┬Ę Hint "Part 2: prove succ is injective. Start with `intro i j h`."

1ŌåÆimport Game.Metadata
     2ŌåÆ
     3ŌåÆWorld "FinTuples"
     4ŌåÆLevel 1
     5ŌåÆ
     6ŌåÆTitle "Functions as Tuples"
     7ŌåÆ
     8ŌåÆIntroduction "
     9ŌåÆ# Functions from Fin as Tuples
    10ŌåÆ
    11ŌåÆIn the previous worlds, you learned that `Fin n` is the type of
    12ŌåÆnatural numbers less than `n`. Now comes a key insight:
    13ŌåÆ
    14ŌåÆ**A function `Fin n ŌåÆ ╬▒` is the same thing as an `n`-tuple** ŌĆö an
    15ŌåÆordered list of `n` elements from `╬▒`.
    16ŌåÆ
    17ŌåÆWhy? A function from `Fin 3 ŌåÆ ŌäĢ` is determined by three values:
    18ŌåÆwhat it returns at `0`, at `1`, and at `2`. Those three values, in
    19ŌåÆorder, form a triple. Conversely, any triple $(a_0, a_1, a_2)$
    20ŌåÆdefines a function `Fin 3 ŌåÆ ŌäĢ` by $i \\mapsto a_i$.
    21ŌåÆ
    22ŌåÆIn Lean, `![10, 20, 30]` creates a function `Fin 3 ŌåÆ ŌäĢ` that
    23ŌåÆreturns `10` at index `0`, `20` at index `1`, and `30` at index `2`.
    24ŌåÆThe `!` prefix distinguishes this from a plain list ŌĆö it's a
    25ŌåÆ**vector** (a function from `Fin n`), not a linked list.
    26ŌåÆ
    27ŌåÆ**Your task**: Verify that the element at index 1 of `![10, 20, 30]`
    28ŌåÆis `20`. (Remember: indexing starts at 0.)
    29ŌåÆ"
    30ŌåÆ
    31ŌåÆ/-- The element at index 1 of `![10, 20, 30]` is 20. -/
    32ŌåÆStatement : (![10, 20, 30] : Fin 3 ŌåÆ ŌäĢ) 1 = 20 := by
    33ŌåÆ  Hint "The value `![10, 20, 30] 1` reduces to `20` by definition.
    34ŌåÆ  Use `rfl`."
    35ŌåÆ  rfl
    36ŌåÆ
    37ŌåÆConclusion "
    38ŌåÆYou just accessed an element of a 3-tuple. The key idea:
    39ŌåÆ
    40ŌåÆ$$\\texttt{![10, 20, 30]}$$
    41ŌåÆ
    42ŌåÆcreates a function `Fin 3 ŌåÆ ŌäĢ`. Evaluating it at index `1` gives
    43ŌåÆ`20` ŌĆö the second element (counting from 0).
    44ŌåÆ
    45ŌåÆThis is analogous to subscript notation in mathematics:
    46ŌåÆif $v = (10, 20, 30)$, then $v_1 = 20$.
    47ŌåÆIn Lean, `v 1` plays the role of $v_1$.
    48ŌåÆ
    49ŌåÆSince the value is determined by the definition, `rfl` closes
    50ŌåÆthe equation.
    51ŌåÆ
    52ŌåÆNotice the type: `Fin 3 ŌåÆ ŌäĢ`. The domain `Fin 3` has exactly 3
    53ŌåÆelements, so the function is completely determined by 3 values ŌĆö
    54ŌåÆit's a 3-tuple. More generally, `Fin n ŌåÆ ╬▒` is an `n`-tuple. The
    55ŌåÆnumber of elements in the tuple IS the number of elements in `Fin n`.
    56ŌåÆThis connection between tuple length and `Fin n` cardinality will be
    57ŌåÆformalized in the Cardinality world.
    58ŌåÆ"
    59ŌåÆ

    65ŌåÆ
    66ŌåÆTitle "Vec Notation with Variables"
    67ŌåÆ
    68ŌåÆIntroduction "
    69ŌåÆ# Tuples Work with Variables Too
    70ŌåÆ
    71ŌåÆThe `![...]` notation doesn't just work with concrete numbers.
    72ŌåÆYou can use variables:
    73ŌåÆ
    74ŌåÆIf `a b c : ŌäĢ`, then `![a, b, c] : Fin 3 ŌåÆ ŌäĢ` is a 3-tuple
    75ŌåÆwhose values are `a`, `b`, and `c`.
    76ŌåÆ
    77ŌåÆElement access still reduces by definition:
    78ŌåÆ- `![a, b, c] 0 = a`
    79ŌåÆ- `![a, b, c] 1 = b`
    80ŌåÆ- `![a, b, c] 2 = c`
    81ŌåÆ
    82ŌåÆThese hold by `rfl` because `![a, b, c]` is *defined* to return
    83ŌåÆ`a` at index 0, `b` at index 1, and `c` at index 2 ŌĆö regardless
    84ŌåÆof what `a`, `b`, `c` actually are.
    85ŌåÆ
    86ŌåÆ**Your task**: Prove that the first element of `![a, b, c]` is `a`.
    87ŌåÆ"
    88ŌåÆ
    89ŌåÆ/-- Accessing the first element of a variable tuple. -/
    90ŌåÆStatement (a b c : ŌäĢ) : (![a, b, c] : Fin 3 ŌåÆ ŌäĢ) 0 = a := by
    91ŌåÆ  Hint "Even with variables, element access is definitional. Use `rfl`."
    92ŌåÆ  rfl
    93ŌåÆ
    94ŌåÆConclusion "
    95ŌåÆThe pattern: `![a, b, c] i` reduces to the `i`-th element by
    96ŌåÆdefinition.
    97ŌåÆ
    98ŌåÆYou can build tuples of any length and with any type:
    99ŌåÆ- `![5] : Fin 1 ŌåÆ ŌäĢ` ŌĆö a 1-tuple
   100ŌåÆ- `![true, false] : Fin 2 ŌåÆ Bool` ŌĆö a Bool pair
   101ŌåÆ- `![1, 2, 3, 4] : Fin 4 ŌåÆ ŌäĢ` ŌĆö a 4-tuple
   102ŌåÆ
   103ŌåÆThe type annotation (`: Fin n ŌåÆ ╬▒`) is sometimes needed to
   104ŌåÆhelp Lean figure out the types.
   105ŌåÆ
   106ŌåÆIn the next level, you'll learn to *build* tuples using
   107ŌåÆpattern-matching syntax.
   108ŌåÆ"

   116ŌåÆTitle "Building Tuples"
   117ŌåÆ
   118ŌåÆIntroduction "
   119ŌåÆ# Building a Tuple Step by Step
   120ŌåÆ
   121ŌåÆYou've seen how to *read* tuples. Now let's *build* one.
   122ŌåÆ
   123ŌåÆThe goal asks: does there exist a function `Fin 3 ŌåÆ ŌäĢ` that maps
   124ŌåÆ`0` to `10`, `1` to `20`, and `2` to `30`?
   125ŌåÆ
   126ŌåÆYou already know such a function: `![10, 20, 30]`. The strategy:
   127ŌåÆ
   128ŌåÆ1. **`use ![10, 20, 30]`** ŌĆö provide the witness
   129ŌåÆ2. **`constructor`** ŌĆö split the conjunction `P Ōł¦ Q` into two goals
   130ŌåÆ3. **`rfl`** ŌĆö each equation holds by definition
   131ŌåÆ
   132ŌåÆThis is the same `constructor` + `rfl` pattern you'll use
   133ŌåÆthroughout this world whenever you need to verify properties of
   134ŌåÆa specific tuple.
   135ŌåÆ
   136ŌåÆ**Your task**: Build `![10, 20, 30]` and verify its values.
   137ŌåÆ"
   138ŌåÆ
   139ŌåÆ/-- A function `Fin 3 ŌåÆ ŌäĢ` that maps 0 to 10, 1 to 20, and 2 to 30. -/
   140ŌåÆStatement : Ōłā f : Fin 3 ŌåÆ ŌäĢ, f 0 = 10 Ōł¦ f 1 = 20 Ōł¦ f 2 = 30 := by
   141ŌåÆ  Hint "Provide the tuple with `use ![10, 20, 30]`."
   142ŌåÆ  use ![10, 20, 30]
   143ŌåÆ  Hint "The goal is now `![10, 20, 30] 0 = 10 Ōł¦ ...`.
   144ŌåÆ  Use `constructor` to split the conjunction."
   145ŌåÆ  constructor
   146ŌåÆ  ┬Ę Hint "The goal is `![10, 20, 30] 0 = 10`. Use `rfl`."
   147ŌåÆ    rfl
   148ŌåÆ  ┬Ę Hint "Use `constructor` to split the remaining conjunction."
   149ŌåÆ    constructor
   150ŌåÆ    ┬Ę Hint "Use `rfl`."
   151ŌåÆ      rfl
   152ŌåÆ    ┬Ę Hint "Use `rfl`."
   153ŌåÆ      rfl
   154ŌåÆ
   155ŌåÆConclusion "
   156ŌåÆYou built a 3-tuple and verified its values step by step:
   157ŌåÆ
